--- Thread (50 messages, 2018-03-07) ---
[2018-03-07 23:12] mtanzer: I don't mind units that make you think but the thinking should have a significant influence on the outcome of the games
[2018-03-07 23:13] mrguy888: Sometimes it gets you an extra hp.
[2018-03-07 23:13] Deleted User (Deleted User): isn't that an autobuy if you would otherwise float a red
[2018-03-07 23:13] mqp: the most fun part about prismata is thinking harder and harder about smaller and smaller margins of expected value
[2018-03-07 23:13] mtanzer: like for example the decisions about how many pixies to click when you have 20 of them and villain has 10 engineers are the types of decisions I don't want in the game
[2018-03-07 23:13] Deleted User (Deleted User): huh. u just click all of them
[2018-03-07 23:13] mqp: yeah that decision is very rarely hard
[2018-03-07 23:14] mtanzer: it's an easy one but I think it also gets my point across?
[2018-03-07 23:14] iminabearsuit: You click 3.5 pixies every turn
[2018-03-07 23:14] mtanzer: apparently not so easy that trump can get it right though lol
[2018-03-07 23:14] wilson0862 (Wilson): I mean... his play isn't that much worse.
[2018-03-07 23:14] wilson0862 (Wilson): Which is why you didn't want that to really be a decision point, presumably.
[2018-03-07 23:15] mtanzer: yeah it's not worse at all in the vast majority of games that's why I don't like the decision existing
[2018-03-07 23:15] wilson0862 (Wilson): Which is why cooledcannon doesn't believe in 45s. It matters but only sometimes and not enough. 🤔
[2018-03-07 23:15] mtanzer: hearthstone had a lot of these sorts of decisions regarding unit placement
[2018-03-07 23:15] wilson0862 (Wilson): So he doesn't think about it.
[2018-03-07 23:15] Deleted User (Deleted User): ok sure u keep it if frontline or chill or some shit
[2018-03-07 23:16] mtanzer: where the order you place units on your side of the board would only matter in the case of like 1 or 2 specific cards in the game which your opponent was extremely unlikely to have
[2018-03-07 23:16] mtanzer: those always annoyed me too
[2018-03-07 23:16] wilson0862 (Wilson): We're mostly talking about minimizing opponent's granularity I think @Deleted User
[2018-03-07 23:16] wilson0862 (Wilson): At some hypothetical future point.
[2018-03-07 23:16] Deleted User (Deleted User): yeah so if you're killing 10 engineers then u wipe them out I think
[2018-03-07 23:17] mtanzer: you'd click 10 pixies to kill 10 engis but not 20
[2018-03-07 23:17] mtanzer: because if you click all 20 some pixies are killing walls and forcefields, and those might be able to kill granularity on future turns
[2018-03-07 23:17] wilson0862 (Wilson): Slight optimization decision.
[2018-03-07 23:17] Deleted User (Deleted User): would you keep a stack of 20 pixies if you could kill 10 Es though?
[2018-03-07 23:18] mtanzer: in theory you usually want to kill the maximum number of engis you can (often 2 vs wall absorb) in spots like that
[2018-03-07 23:18] Deleted User (Deleted User): instinctively I think no
[2018-03-07 23:18] mtanzer: without spending pixies to kill non-engis
[2018-03-07 23:18] wilson0862 (Wilson): You want to maximize granularity killed and minimize pixie clicks theoretically.
[2018-03-07 23:18] mqp: you can tell that games are designed by people who aren't serious about playing games well when they include mechanics that boil down to "memorize the probability distribution for all hidden information in the game, then we'll talk"
[2018-03-07 23:19] mqp: e.g. many CCGs, innovation come to mind
[2018-03-07 23:20] wilson0862 (Wilson): That applies to both, no?
[2018-03-07 23:20] mqp: poker is a different story because it does that mechanic well -- the prior distribution is really simple and you can hold it in your head, so you only need to figure out how to interpret your opponent's actions
[2018-03-07 23:20] mqp: it's tractable to think about without expending a ton of up-front memorization effort
[2018-03-07 23:21] mqp: with MTG, if i want to be competitive in a draft, i need to memorize more or less the whole set that i'm drafting
[2018-03-07 23:21] iminabearsuit: I do think Hearthstone is notoriously bad on it, compared to Magic. Secrets being a prime example.
[2018-03-07 23:21] Deleted User (Deleted User): I think you can use a deck tracker no?
[2018-03-07 23:21] mtanzer: even when I was playing competitively I would still forget some of the rarely-played secrets sometimes
[2018-03-07 23:21] wilson0862 (Wilson): You can't use a deck tracker until you're done drafting though.
[2018-03-07 23:22] wilson0862 (Wilson): mq is talking about the actual draft process right?
[2018-03-07 23:22] iminabearsuit: No, combat tricks primarly.
[2018-03-07 23:22] wilson0862 (Wilson): Like I'm picking this card in a vacuum but what's the chance it will have support by the end of the draft?
[2018-03-07 23:22] mqp: well that was just an example, both the draft process and actual gameplay are hard to do well unless you memorized all this
[2018-03-07 23:23] iminabearsuit: If opponent has 3 open mana, which instants can he have. Whats more likely, and can I play around multiples
[2018-03-07 23:23] mqp: in my limited hearthstone experience it felt like nearly 100% of the decision-making relied on this memorization, because in isolation it's so trivial to figure out what the most efficient use of your mana and efficient exchanges are
[2018-03-07 23:23] wilson0862 (Wilson): ya, it's the same issue. True.
[2018-03-07 23:23] mqp: so the only thing to know about is what things are likely to be in your opponent's deck
[2018-03-07 23:23] wilson0862 (Wilson): @ draft vs play in MTG
[2018-03-07 23:24] wilson0862 (Wilson): ya and that would matter more in HS if the meta was more diverse.
